summary: McKenzie dissects a case study where an RSS reader developer's aggressive product activation system and accusatory customer service email turned a deliriously happy customer into a vocal public critic. The post traces how a paying customer who actively recommended the product was flagged by activation code, then received an email implying license breach, leading to a Digg-amplified PR disaster. McKenzie argues the developer had multiple opportunities to avoid the situation: using better activation methods, accepting some key abuse as a cost of business, or simply responding with trust and grace. He provides example emails showing how to handle activation concerns without accusation, demonstrating that assuming good faith costs nothing while suspicion can cost everything.
key_points: Product activation systems that punish legitimate customers are a bigger business risk than the piracy they aim to prevent
Customer service emails should lead with trust and helpfulness, never with accusation or legal-sounding language like 'breach of license'
When you've wronged a customer publicly, the recovery must be proportional: offer a refund, a free license, and an apology rather than doubling down
Words like 'suspiciously' and 'breach of license' in customer communications are relationship-ending moves that can cascade into PR disasters
The same factual concern (multiple activations) can be raised as a helpful inquiry rather than an accusation, with dramatically different outcomes
money_quotes: I find money salves ego pretty well. Its like a cold compress made of cash, and without good customer service you have less cash to compress!
The author could have accepted that some keys get abused as a cost of doing business.
If you absolutely must continue, let trust be your watchword.

Insinuating that if there is anyone to blame it is probably you costs you absolutely nothing but defuses a potentially volatile situation a lot more than words like suspiciously and breach of license.
I don't know what happens next but its sure not '… Profit!'
key_insight: Treating paying customers as suspected pirates costs far more in lost goodwill and public reputation than any piracy ever would, so default to trust in every customer interaction.
